item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The first image that is used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Source: SCP Foundation Wiki
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: 1572-1.jpg
Author: Photosynthetic
Release year: 2013

The second image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY 2.0
Title: Dead sea monster
Author: Mujib MK
Release year: 2006

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As repeatedly stated before, this one have a fairly good concept, but in the end does nothing at all with it. I decided to downvote in the hopes that someday it will get a new rewrite.

1- Demolishing the places is a method for what? Why should someone even care about stopping the event when it isn't understood but a fair way to stop it is already known?

2- I'm not a fan of wasting D-Classes, but to stop testing altogether just because half a dozen D-Class got lost?

3- Subjects can or will try to reach the area? The effects of the object are quickly glanced over without any true clinical analysis. There are several questions, and they are questions that clearly should be answered at this point, not mysteries.

4- When hallucinations start? When the entities appear? What kind of test was done with them? They really seem like just an excuse for the photo because they do nothing at the end and their implication is not investigated.

5- Five tests are shown, and they are executed in a nearly identical fashion. They reveal new data for sheer luck, because they are not prepared to collect that data.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though this could use a bit more fleshing out, the concept and execution is interesting enough for me to yield an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going with a no-vote on this.

I really like the premise, and the first part of the article is really well-written and has some good hooks, but as a few folks above have mentioned, the testing logs don't really add much (and might even detract), and there isn't a lot of payoff in the end, either.

This feels like it could be fleshed out a lot and turned into something really unique and amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The childhood memory hook makes for an interesting twist, but not enough is done with it. In the end, this really feels like a thing that kills you.

The first picture is also pretty useless, and the second one, though perplexing and worrisome, actually adds nothing to the document. Although the creatures are referred to, they ultimately add little to the feel.

This could use some definite fleshing out.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel this wouldve been better without the test log. I'm staying neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus, this whole backroots mythos is so reminiscent of my own childhood. I don't know how, but somehow I suspect it's so ingenious that it triggers the same kind of feeling in many other people, or maybe it was just my childhood that had these specific experiences, but I can definitely relive my memories through this series.

Infinite +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I concur.
This could be fixed by mentioning the esclating instructions, threats, GPS units, and remote video equipment given to the progressive D-class. All of which would fail, I'm assuming; but it's in character for the Foundation to make the attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The test log is the same test repeated, with the same results (and methodology) every time, it adds nothing. It would read better with some exposition worked into the log over the course of the test series, I think.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The test logs don't really make any sense to me from an in-universe perspective. After losing even one person you'd think the foundation would at least attempt to somehow secure the second. After losing three they would either cancel testing or try a lot harder to find out where they were disappearing to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's sort of what I was thinking. Does that make sense in this context?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe it's a chunk of whale that washed up on a beach somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tests CC and ZA… Man, they can lose other people there too? I think this is actually quite a bit more interesting than the original "backroots" article, and certainly expands it while standing alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the second picture supposed to be? I don't know about some of the D-class, but that doesn't look like a manifestation of any of my childhood memories…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree that the testing log added very little. Only the last one is even a little bit interesting.

Also, the appearance of monsters feels a little forced here; it's like you wrote it, decided it wasn't creepy enough, so monsters were added.

Overall not bad, but I don't feel like its interesting enough on its own. Reading the comments below, it sounds like this is part of something bigger, which is cool, but I would almost rather this be a foundation tale than as an SCP, at least in its current form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have any strong opinions on this either way. It's not bad, but it isn't particularly interesting, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [purged]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An addition to the mythos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this a rewrite, or in addition to the other one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat, the first picture makes no sense to be there, if they're just traveling along normal roads. It's just a picture of a roadside. A mundane, non-anomalous roadside that happens, in one case, to be along the route chosen to take you to the root.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't think that the testing log adds much of anything to the actual article, but the main body was interesting enough to me, so +1
